english: W. *kú-lig-gi (ku lig gi) tsaṅs (tshangs)*, key-hole, col. for mtsams (mtshams) (?).

(evid. preterite of ₒtsaṅ-ba ('tshang ba)) 1. purified, clean, pure, holy, tsaṅs-par (tshangs par) gyur (gyur) ċig (cig) prob. be clean! be forgiven! Dzl. 72, 13; gyod-tsaṅs (gyod tshangs), mtol-tsaṅs (mthol tshangs), v. the two; tsaṅs-par (tshangs par) spyód-pa (spyod pa), tsaṅs-pai (tshangs pa'i) spyód-pa (spyod pa) spyód-pa (spyod pa), tsaṅs-par (tshangs par) mtsuṅs-par (mtshungs par) spyód-pa (spyod pa) 1. to be clean, chaste, holy, to do what is right, to lead an honest, upright life. 2. to be a priest, to belong to a holy order, and as sbst. priest, cleric; mi-tsaṅs-par (mi tshangs par) spyód-pa (spyod pa), not to be clean, chaste etc., esp. with bud-méd-la (bud med la) to commit one's self with a woman Mil. -- tsaṅs-skúd (tshangs skud), Sch.: 'holy cord, the bond of spirits' (?) -- tsaṅs-tíg (tshangs thig) equator, prob. of Cs.'s construction, cf. dguṅ (dgung) extr. -- 2. Brahma, an Indian deity transplanted into Buddhism; he is occasionally called lha (lha) ċén-po (chen po) (Glr.) and proverbial for his melodious voice, yet otherwise not of any consequence. -- tsaṅs-pai (tshangs pa'i) bú-ga (bu ga) = mtsóg-ma (mtshog ma) Med., Pth.

(cf. tsod (tshod)) 1. measure, a. in a general sense, size: ċe-ċuṅ-gi (che chung gi) tsád-la (tshad la) according to the size, in size Glr.; mi-tsad (mi tshad) size of a (full-grown) man Tar.; sku-tsád (sku tshad) stature, size of body, resp. Glr.; zlá-bai (zla ba'i) dkyil-ₒkór-gyi (dkyil 'khor gyi) tsad (tshad) the size of the moon's disk Stg.; stobs (stobs) gyad (gyad) stóbs-po-ċei (stobs po che'i) tsád-du (tshad du) pyin-te (phyin te) his strength was equal to that of a powerful athlete Dzl.; *ṭu (khru) súm-ċui (sum cu'i) tsad (tshad) ċo (co) gos (gos)* W. make it thirty cubits in size; ḱam-tsád-du (kham tshad du) gcód-pa (gcod pa) to cut into bits piecemeal Dzl.; ċu-rgyún (chu rgyun) ḱyab-tsád-du (khyab tshad du) as far as the waters covered it Tar.; ṅóm-tsad (ngom tshad) (-du) ₒtúṅ-ba ('thung ba) to drink one's fill; gnás-tsad (gnas tshad) seems to express chronology Wdk.; mnan-tsad (mnan tshad) direction how the pulse is to be felt (or pressed) Med.; ḱyéd-rnams-kyi (khyed rnams kyi) ċós-bslab-tsad (chos bslab tshad) according to your view of religious studies Mil.; dró-tsad (dro tshad) thermometer, graṅ-droi (grang dro'i) tsad (tshad) id.; yaṅ-lċii (yang lci'i) tsad (tshad) barometer; mto-dman-gyi (mtho dman gyi) tsad (tshad) scale for the rising and falling (of the barometer); all these appear to be proposals of Cs. for the respective physical terms; pa-tsád (pha tshad) distance (v. sub pa (pha) II); tsad-méd (tshad med) (-pa) unmeasured, immeasurable, innumerable, e.g. yón-tan (yon tan) Dzl.; tsad-med (tshad med) (-pa) bźi (bzhi) the four immeasurables (viz. merits): byáms-pa (byams pa), sñiṅ-rje (snying rje), dká-ba (dka' ba) and btaṅ-sñóm (btang snyom) Dom., spyod-pa (spyod pa) to practise them, tob-pa (thob pa) to attain to them Dzl.; ṅa-bas (nga bas) mi (mi) tsad (tshad) gźan (gzhan) yaṅ (yang) an infinity of others besides me Mil. b. the full measure, which is not short of the proper quantity, standard, tsád-du (tshad du) pyin-pa (phyin pa), skyé-ba (skye ba) (Sch. also ₒḱyól-ba ('khyol ba)) to grow, so as to reach the proper measure; tsád-du (tshad du) skyés-pa (skyes pa) grown up, full-sized, adj. Dzl.; *tse' (tshe') żág-pa (zhag pa)* to set up a pattern, or as a pattern C. tsad-ldán (tshad ldan) right (as weight), about the same as 'gaged', just, fair, with regard to persons (ni f.) C. c. the right measure, which does not exceed the proper quantity: tsád-gcòd-pa (tshad gcod pa) to limit, bed-ċód (bed chod) the enjoyment Mil.; bza-btúṅ-la (bza' btung la) to observe the proper measure in eating and drinking, *tse' (tshe') ₒdzím-pa ('dzim pa)*, or żág-pa (zhag pa) C. id.; tsád-las (tshad las) ₒdá-ba ('da' ba), tál-ba (thal ba) to exceed the proper measure frq.; yid-pám-pa-la (yid pham pa la) tsád-las (tshad las) ₒdás-pa ('das pa) yoṅ (yong) the dejection increases to an excess Mil. -- To 1, a. may be referred d. those instances in which the word assuming the character of an affix serves to form abstract nouns, such as gdeṅs-tsád (gdengs tshad), or rtogs-tsád (rtogs tshad), Mil. in several passages (cf. also tsod (tshod)) further to 1, b may be reckoned e. the signification all, dgé-ba (dge ba) byed (byed) tsad (tshad) all the pious Pth., to which also Tar. 54, 15 may be referred; sna-tsád (sna tshad) of every kind, of all sorts Glr.; *że (zhe) tse' (tshe') ċu'-du (cu' du) soṅ (song)* C. all his eating agreed with him extremely well; ₒdir ('dir) ldóm-bu-ba (ldom bu ba) byuṅ (byung) tsad (tshad) all the beggars that show themselves here Mil.; mi (mi) yoṅs (yongs) tsad (tshad) all the people that come; snaṅ (snang) tsad (tshad) ċós-skur (chos skur) śar (shar) all that happens appears as ċós-sku (chos sku) Glr.; gsuṅ (gsung) tsad (tshad) all that is ordered, proclaimed Sch.; tsogs (tshogs) tsád (tshad) all the people assembled Sch.; and f. enough, esp with a negation: ₒdra-ba ('dra ba) mi (mi) tsad (tshad) not having enough of the comparisons, not resting satisfied with them; *ma (ma) tsád-de (tshad de)* W. = ma (ma) zád-de (zad de) B. not only. -- 2. a certain definite measure, in compounds: dpag-tsád (dpag tshad) a mile, sor-tsád (sor tshad) an inch: also pleon. kru-tsad (khru tshad) an ell Cs. = kru (khru). -- 3. goal, mark, the point to which racers run C.
